cases.summary: Court dismissed CIPA wiretapping claim against Build.com, finding plaintiff failed to adequately allege third-party eavesdropping by Salesforce in website chat feature.

holdings.text: Under CIPA § 631(a), Clause One does not apply to internet communications, as the statute's plain language limits coverage to 'telegraph or telephone' communications.

To state an aiding and abetting claim under CIPA § 631(a) Clause Four, plaintiff must plausibly allege that a third-party service provider violated one of the first three clauses and that defendant aided that violation.

The party exemption under CIPA does not extend to third-party software providers who have the capability to use recorded communications for their own purposes, following the reasoning in Javier v. Assurance IQ.

cases.summary: Court dismissed CIPA wiretapping claims against Noom and FullStory, holding session replay software provider was Noom's vendor, not third-party eavesdropper.

holdings.text: A software vendor that provides session replay services to capture and analyze a website owner's data acts as an extension of the website owner, not as a third-party eavesdropper, under CIPA section 631(a)

Session replay software that records website interactions for the benefit of the website owner does not constitute illegal wiretapping when the vendor functions as a service provider rather than an independent data aggregator
Personal jurisdiction over a software vendor requires more than merely providing services to a company with substantial forum contacts; the vendor must engage in purposeful direction toward the forum

cases.summary: Court dismissed CIPA claims against Sephora for recording website chats, ruling the party exception protects direct communication participants and section 632.7 doesn't cover internet communications.

holdings.text: Under CIPA section 631(a), the 'party exception' protects direct participants to communications from wiretapping liability, as only third parties can 'secretly' listen to conversations between other parties.
CIPA section 632.7 applies only to communications between specific types of telephone devices and does not extend to internet-based chat communications, even when accessed via smartphone.
For CIPA section 631(a) clause 2 violations, communications must be intercepted 'during transmission' and 'in transit,' not after completion or while in storage.

cases.summary: Court dismissed most claims in putative class action against Nike and FullStory over website tracking software but allowed claim that Nike aided FullStory's alleged wiretapping to proceed.

holdings.text: A third-party software provider does not become a 'party' to communications between a website user and the website operator merely by providing recording and transmission services for the website operator, and can therefore be liable for wiretapping under CIPA § 631(a).

A website operator can be held liable under CIPA § 631(a) for aiding, agreeing with, or conspiring with a third party to intercept communications, even if the operator was a party to the underlying communication.
Session replay software that records keystrokes and creates video recordings of user interactions can capture 'contents' of communications sufficient to state a claim under CIPA § 631(a).
Mere possession of wiretapping software without allegations of concrete injury from that possession fails to establish Article III standing for claims under CIPA § 635.

cases.summary: Court denied motion to dismiss CIPA claims against AI voice assistant company that recorded restaurant orders, finding it was third party with capability to use data for own purposes.

holdings.text: Under the capability approach, a software provider is a third party subject to CIPA section 631 if it has the capability to use intercepted communications for its own purposes beyond merely serving its clients, even if it also functions as a service provider

Allegations of sharing personally identifiable information and personal financial information during a phone call are sufficient to establish a confidential communication under CIPA section 632 at the pleading stage
AI voice assistant software that intercepts phone calls and directly engages with callers in real-time constitutes interception of communications 'in transit' under CIPA section 631

cases.summary: Court dismissed CIPA claims against Keurig for allegedly intercepting website chat communications, finding the statutes don't cover internet communications and plaintiff failed to adequately plead third-party eavesdropping.
cases.caseName: Valenzuela v. Keurig Green Mountain, Inc.

holdings.text: CIPA Section 631(a)'s first clause, which prohibits unauthorized connections with 'telegraph or telephone wire, line, cable, or instrument,' does not apply to internet communications by its plain terms, even when accessed via smartphone.
Under CIPA Section 631(a)'s second clause, a plaintiff must provide factual detail beyond conclusory allegations about how real-time interception occurs, not merely restate the legal elements.

CIPA Section 632.7 only applies to communications between specified types of telephones (cellular, landline, cordless) and does not extend to internet chat communications, despite the statute's broad definition of 'communications.'

cases.summary: Court denied motion to dismiss CIPA claims against aquarium supplier whose website chat feature allegedly allowed third-party interception of customer communications for advertising purposes.
cases.caseName: Rodriguez v. Aquatic Sales Solutions LLC

holdings.text: A defendant that operates an interactive website with chat functionality and makes substantial sales to forum state residents (12%+ of total sales) expressly aims its conduct at the forum under the Calder effects test for specific personal jurisdiction

A third-party software vendor that intercepts communications and uses the data for its own profit (such as providing to other companies for advertising) rather than solely for the merchant's benefit constitutes a third-party eavesdropper under CIPA rather than merely an extension of the merchant
Allegations that chat communications are routed through a third party's servers and intercepted in real time are sufficient to establish interception 'while in transit' under CIPA's second clause

cases.summary: Court dismissed CIPA and intrusion claims against Bloomingdale's for website session replay tracking, finding website interactions aren't communication 'contents' and users lack privacy expectations on defendant's own site.

holdings.text: Website interactions such as button clicks, mouse movements, scrolling, and key presses constitute 'record information' rather than the 'contents' of communications under CIPA Section 631, similar to federal wiretap law precedent
Users do not have a reasonable expectation of privacy in browsing data collected on a defendant's own website, distinguishing cases involving cross-site tracking after users logged out

Personal jurisdiction exists where a website is integrated with defendant's forum state operations, even when claims arise from website activity, if the website acts as a conduit to brick-and-mortar operations

cases.summary: Class action against GoodRx and tech companies for sharing health data through tracking pixels survived motion to dismiss on most claims.

holdings.text: At the motion to dismiss stage, plaintiffs need not allege specific technical details about tracking technologies' inner workings that only defendants would possess
Health-related communications with medical providers constitute confidential communications under CIPA
Broad privacy policy disclosures about general data collection, when read alongside specific promises not to share health data, do not establish consent to health data sharing as a matter of law
The discovery rule can apply to highly technical privacy violations where plaintiffs allege they could not have discovered the conduct without the benefit of discovery

cases.summary: Ninth Circuit affirmed remand of privacy class actions against Cedars-Sinai, rejecting federal officer removal based on HITECH Act compliance for patient portal with tracking software.
cases.caseName: John Doe v. Cedars-Sinai Health System

holdings.text: Healthcare providers that develop patient portal websites in compliance with HITECH Act and Meaningful Use Program requirements are not 'acting under' a federal officer for purposes of federal officer removal under 28 U.S.C. § 1442(a)(1).
Mere compliance with comprehensive federal regulatory schemes, even when accompanied by governmental supervision and monitoring, does not establish federal officer jurisdiction for removal purposes.
The design and operation of private websites is not a 'basic governmental task' that would support federal officer removal, even when the website furthers broad governmental policy goals.
Receipt of federal incentive payments for regulatory compliance, without more, is insufficient to establish the delegation of legal authority required for federal officer removal.

cases.summary: Court partially granted motion to dismiss healthcare data privacy class action involving Meta Pixel tracking, allowing most claims to proceed while dismissing some under first-to-file rule.

holdings.text: Tracking pixels that capture user search queries and transmit them as full-string URLs containing personal health information constitute interception of 'contents' of communications under CIPA
Communications are intercepted 'in transit' under CIPA when tracking technology simultaneously duplicates and transmits data in real time during the communication process
The ECPA's crime-tort exception applies when healthcare providers intercept communications for the purpose of violating HIPAA, even if primarily motivated by financial gain
Personal health information searched for and entered on healthcare provider websites and patient portals constitutes 'medical information' under the CMIA when combined with identifying information
Patients have a reasonable expectation of privacy in detailed URLs containing their health-related searches and communications with healthcare providers

cases.summary: Court denied Google's motion to dismiss class action claims alleging illegal collection of tax filers' financial data through tracking tools on tax preparation websites.

holdings.text: Under California Penal Code § 631, a third-party tracking service that reads and uses collected data for its own benefit is not merely a passive vendor but can be liable for wiretapping, even if some processing is done for the benefit of website operators.
The mere existence of privacy policies and terms of service cannot establish user consent as a matter of law at the pleading stage when plaintiffs specifically allege they did not consent.

California courts have never recognized a legal presumption that internet communications are not confidential under Penal Code § 632, and sensitive financial information submitted to tax preparation websites can be reasonably expected to remain confidential.

Section 637.2 of the California Invasion of Privacy Act provides a private right of action for violations of Section 635 (eavesdropping device manufacture), as injuries from device use are traceable to the device's creation and provision.

cases.summary: Court dismissed VPPA claims against monster truck company, finding it wasn't a 'video tape service provider' and plaintiffs weren't 'consumers' under the statute.

holdings.text: To qualify as a 'video tape service provider' under the VPPA, a defendant's product or service must be more than 'peripherally or passively involved in the delivery of video content' and must be 'substantially involved in the conveyance of video content' and 'significantly tailored to serve that purpose.'

A 'subscription' under the VPPA requires some factual nexus or relationship between the subscription provided by the defendant and the defendant's allegedly actionable video content; signing up for an email newsletter separate from and after viewing videos is insufficient to establish consumer status.

cases.summary: Court denied Google's summary judgment motion in class action over collection of private browsing data, finding genuine disputes over user consent and standing for privacy violations.

holdings.text: For consent to be actual under privacy statutes, the disclosures must 'explicitly notify' users of the practice at issue, and consent is only effective if the person consented 'to the particular conduct, or to substantially the same conduct.'

A general disclosure about data collection practices is insufficient to establish explicit consent for the specific collection of private browsing data when the company has made representations distinguishing private browsing mode from regular browsing.

Plaintiffs have Article III standing for privacy violations where they allege collection and storage of sensitive browsing data without consent, even if the data is anonymized, when statutory claims codify substantive rights to privacy.
Under California law, breach of contract alone can confer Article III standing where plaintiffs seek actual damages and injunctive relief, not merely nominal damages.

Health-related communications with medical providers constitute confidential communications under CIPA
Broad privacy policy disclosures about general data collection, when read alongside specific promises not to share health data, do not establish consent to health data sharing as a matter of law

cases.summary: District court dismissed CIPA claims against Ford for website chat interception, finding insufficient allegations that third-party LivePerson acted beyond Ford's agent and that internet chats don't qualify as telephone communications under section 632.7.

holdings.text: Under CIPA section 631(a), importing a 'use' requirement into the second clause would render the third clause superfluous, and the focus should be on whether the third party has the capability to use intercepted communications for its own purposes rather than the third party's actual use or intentions.

CIPA section 632.7 applies only to communications between various types of telephones and does not encompass web-based messages sent from internet browsers of smartphones, even when one party uses a smartphone to access the internet.

To establish aiding and abetting liability under CIPA section 631(a), a plaintiff must allege that the defendant knew the third party's conduct constituted a breach of duty, not merely that the defendant knew the third party was capturing communications for the defendant's benefit.

cases.summary: Court denied Zillow's motion to dismiss VPPA and CIPA claims alleging disclosure of users' video viewing activity to third-party trackers like Facebook without consent.

holdings.text: A company can qualify as a 'video tape service provider' under the VPPA when video content delivery is significantly tailored to and essential for its business model, even if the videos are used to facilitate other services like real estate transactions.

A user can be a 'subscriber' under the VPPA without payment if they register for an account and provide personal information, establishing a relationship materially different from casual web browsing.
Allegations that tracking technologies are embedded in websites and intercept communications 'whenever' users interact with content are sufficient to plead the 'in transit' requirement under CIPA.

cases.summary: Ninth Circuit affirmed remand of privacy class actions against Cedars-Sinai, rejecting federal officer removal based on HITECH Act compliance for patient portal with tracking software.
cases.caseName: John Doe v. Cedars-Sinai Health System

holdings.text: Healthcare providers that develop patient portal websites in compliance with HITECH Act and Meaningful Use Program requirements are not 'acting under' a federal officer for purposes of federal officer removal under 28 U.S.C. § 1442(a)(1).
Mere compliance with comprehensive federal regulatory schemes, even when accompanied by governmental supervision and monitoring, does not establish federal officer jurisdiction for removal purposes.
The design and operation of private websites is not a 'basic governmental task' that would support federal officer removal, even when the website furthers broad governmental policy goals.
Receipt of federal incentive payments for regulatory compliance, without more, is insufficient to establish the delegation of legal authority required for federal officer removal.

cases.summary: Court dismissed California privacy claims against Audible for tracking audiobook activity, ruling that contractual choice-of-law provision requiring Washington law to govern was enforceable.

holdings.text: A website's sign-in page provides reasonably conspicuous notice of terms when it clearly states that by signing in, users agree to conditions of use, with a prominent hyperlink directly below the action button, even if accessed repeatedly rather than just once during sign-up.

Terms of use that clearly and unequivocally incorporate other documents by reference in prominent locations, including specific reference to governing law provisions, are enforceable even when the choice-of-law provision appears in the incorporated document rather than the primary agreement.

A contractual choice-of-law provision selecting Washington law over California law does not violate fundamental California public policy merely because Washington's Privacy Act provides narrower protection than CIPA, absent specific statutory language or judicial pronouncements establishing the policy as fundamental.

cases.summary: Court partially granted motion to dismiss healthcare data privacy class action involving Meta Pixel tracking, allowing most claims to proceed while dismissing some under first-to-file rule.

holdings.text: Tracking pixels that capture user search queries and transmit them as full-string URLs containing personal health information constitute interception of 'contents' of communications under CIPA
Communications are intercepted 'in transit' under CIPA when tracking technology simultaneously duplicates and transmits data in real time during the communication process
The ECPA's crime-tort exception applies when healthcare providers intercept communications for the purpose of violating HIPAA, even if primarily motivated by financial gain
Personal health information searched for and entered on healthcare provider websites and patient portals constitutes 'medical information' under the CMIA when combined with identifying information
Patients have a reasonable expectation of privacy in detailed URLs containing their health-related searches and communications with healthcare providers

cases.summary: Court denied motion to dismiss CIPA pen register claim against Mashable for embedding third-party website trackers that collected users' IP addresses and device identifiers without court authorization.

holdings.text: CIPA's pen register provisions apply to website-embedded trackers that record addressing information from electronic communications, extending beyond traditional telephone surveillance to include software processes that capture IP addresses and device identifiers

HTTP requests from users' browsers to website servers constitute 'electronic communications' under CIPA's pen register statute, and IP addresses qualify as 'addressing information' within the statutory definition

A defendant can 'install' or 'use' a pen register under CIPA even when third parties technically operate the tracking technology, if the defendant deliberately embedded the trackers and used the resulting data

cases.summary: Court denied Meta's motion to dismiss class action alleging Meta Pixel tracking on DMV website violated DPPA and CIPA by collecting personal information including disability data without consent.

holdings.text: First names, email addresses, and information that someone has applied for a disability parking placard constitute 'personal information' under the DPPA because they are 'facts that can identify an individual' rather than facts that must uniquely identify an individual in every instance

A disabled parking placard application 'pertains to a motor vehicle operator's permit' under the DPPA because disability parking placards are only used in connection with driving, unlike voter registration forms which have no connection to driving

URLs and user interaction data that reveal personal information such as disability status constitute 'contents' of communications under CIPA when they provide substantive and personal information about the user's browsing activities and personal circumstances

cases.summary: Court denied Google's motion to dismiss class action claims alleging illegal collection of tax filers' financial data through tracking tools on tax preparation websites.

holdings.text: Under California Penal Code § 631, a third-party tracking service that reads and uses collected data for its own benefit is not merely a passive vendor but can be liable for wiretapping, even if some processing is done for the benefit of website operators.
The mere existence of privacy policies and terms of service cannot establish user consent as a matter of law at the pleading stage when plaintiffs specifically allege they did not consent.

California courts have never recognized a legal presumption that internet communications are not confidential under Penal Code § 632, and sensitive financial information submitted to tax preparation websites can be reasonably expected to remain confidential.

Section 637.2 of the California Invasion of Privacy Act provides a private right of action for violations of Section 635 (eavesdropping device manufacture), as injuries from device use are traceable to the device's creation and provision.

cases.summary: Court partially granted Meta's motion to dismiss CIPA claims over video viewing data collection, allowing some wiretapping claims to proceed while dismissing others based on distinction between "contents" and "record information."

holdings.text: Under CIPA § 631, video URLs and titles that reveal specific content viewed constitute 'contents' of communications rather than mere 'record information' because they provide significant information about user's browsing history and personal interests.

Form field entries containing customer information such as name, address, and subscriber identity are 'record information' rather than 'contents' under CIPA § 631, even when intercepted during web communications.

Internet communications can have an objectively reasonable expectation of confidentiality under CIPA § 632 when they reveal particular documents or content within websites and when users have not consented to data collection.
A private right of action exists under CIPA § 635 for injuries caused by use of an eavesdropping device that are traceable to the manufacture, sale, and provision of that device.

cases.summary: Court denied motion to dismiss VPPA claim against electronic music festival company for disclosing video viewing data to Facebook and TikTok via tracking pixels.

holdings.text: Under the VPPA's definition of 'consumer,' the term 'goods or services' is not limited to audiovisual materials, as Congress chose to omit an 'audiovisual' clause from the consumer definition unlike the video tape service provider definition.
A plaintiff can qualify as a VPPA 'consumer' through subscription to a newsletter from a video tape service provider, even if the newsletter is not directly related to the video content at issue.

The knowing disclosure requirement under the VPPA can be satisfied where a defendant purposely installs tracking pixels and controls what data is transmitted to third parties, even without actual knowledge of specific user identifiers.

cases.summary: Court dismissed VPPA class action against Coach.com owner, finding luxury retailer insufficiently engaged in video delivery business to qualify as 'video tape service provider' under statute.

holdings.text: To qualify as a 'video tape service provider' under the VPPA, a defendant's business must be 'significantly tailored to serve that purpose' of delivering video content, not merely 'peripherally or passively' engaged in video delivery.

Under the VPPA, violations are not mere 'procedural violations' and every violation of 18 U.S.C. § 2710(b)(1) presents the precise harm Congress sought to protect against, satisfying Article III concreteness requirements.

cases.summary: Court denied Fandom's motion to dismiss VPPA claim over sharing users' video viewing data with Meta via tracking pixels, finding plaintiff stated valid claims.

holdings.text: Streaming video websites qualify as 'video tape service providers' under the VPPA even when providing free content, as long as the website is significantly tailored to deliver video content to consumers.

A Facebook Profile ID constitutes personally identifiable information under the VPPA's 'ordinary person' standard because anyone can use it to locate and view a user's Facebook profile containing identifying details.
Creating an account and providing personal information to access video content establishes a 'subscriber' relationship under the VPPA, even without payment.
Data sharing for advertising analytics purposes does not qualify as 'incident to the ordinary course of business' under the VPPA's exceptions.

cases.summary: Class action alleging Forbes violated VPPA by sharing subscribers' video viewing data with Facebook through tracking pixels without consent.
District court denied Star Tribune's motion to dismiss VPPA class action over Facebook Pixel data sharing, finding plaintiff had Article III standing and stated plausible claims.
cases.caseName: Feldman v. Star Tribune Media Co. LLC

holdings.text: A plaintiff alleging nonconsensual sharing of private video-viewing information has suffered a concrete injury in fact for Article III standing purposes, as this bears a close relationship to the common law tort of intrusion upon seclusion.

Under the VPPA's 'knowingly' requirement, if disclosed information constitutes 'personally identifiable information,' allegations that such information was consciously disclosed suffice - there is no additional requirement that the defendant knew the recipient would actually connect the disclosed information.

The availability of measures a user could take to prevent data disclosure (such as logging out of Facebook or using different browsers) does not establish that any resulting injury was self-inflicted for Article III traceability purposes, absent evidence of actual consent to the disclosure.

cases.summary: District court dismissed VPPA claim against Caesars Entertainment, finding online casino games are not similar enough to video tapes and plaintiff failed to show he was a consumer of audio-visual materials.

holdings.text: Online casino games are not sufficiently similar to video cassette tapes to qualify their provider as a 'video tape service provider' under the VPPA where no prerecorded content is alleged
To qualify as a 'subscriber' and thus 'consumer' under the VPPA, a plaintiff must specifically subscribe to audio-visual materials, not just to newsletters or email communications

Audio-visual material is only similar to video cassette tapes under the VPPA if it includes prerecorded video content, as the adjective 'prerecorded' modifies both 'video cassette tapes' and 'similar audio-visual materials'

cases.summary: Court dismissed VPPA class action against Washington Examiner, finding plaintiff's newsletter subscription insufficient to qualify as 'consumer' under statute despite having standing.
cases.caseName: Pileggi v. Washington Newspaper Publishing Co., LLC

holdings.text: A plaintiff's subscription to a defendant's newsletter does not qualify them as a 'subscriber' under the VPPA where the subscription has no connection to the audio-visual content accessed on the defendant's website and provides no enhanced access to video content.

Alleged unlawful disclosure of private video viewing information to third parties without consent constitutes a concrete injury for Article III standing purposes, as it bears a close relationship to the common law tort of intrusion upon seclusion.
To qualify as a VPPA 'subscriber,' there must be a factual nexus or relationship between the subscription provided by the defendant and the defendant's allegedly actionable video content.

cases.summary: Court partially granted motion to dismiss privacy claims against streaming service MUBI for tracking pixel disclosures, allowing VPPA and state privacy claims to proceed while dismissing UCL and injunctive relief claims.

holdings.text: The disclosure of private video viewing information constitutes an intangible harm sufficient for Article III standing under the VPPA, as every Section 2710(b)(1) violation presents the precise harm Congress sought to protect against.

A sign-in wrap agreement fails to provide reasonably conspicuous notice when it uses tiny font against distracting backgrounds, lacks proper hyperlink formatting, and requires users to scroll significantly to find terms.
Aggregating individually insufficient forms of notice across multiple webpages does not create adequate notice for purposes of establishing user consent.

cases.summary: Court dismissed privacy class action against Ubisoft, finding plaintiffs consented to data sharing with Meta through cookies banner and privacy policy acceptances.

holdings.text: Consent to cookies and data sharing can be established through a combination of cookie banners, privacy policy acceptance, and repeated consent during transactions, without requiring granular disclosures of specific data elements shared with third parties.

Under the VPPA, informed written consent does not require disclosure that specific data combinations like 'video game titles combined with unique Facebook identifiers' will be shared, but rather general disclosure of cookie usage and third-party data sharing.
The ECPA crime-tort exception does not apply when the primary motivation for data collection is economic benefit rather than to commit tortious acts against users.

Privacy policies that inform users about data collection and sharing practices, combined with opt-out mechanisms, can satisfy consent requirements across multiple privacy statutes including VPPA, ECPA, CIPA, and state constitutional privacy claims.

cases.summary: Court denied Zillow's motion to dismiss VPPA and CIPA claims alleging disclosure of users' video viewing activity to third-party trackers like Facebook without consent.

holdings.text: A company can qualify as a 'video tape service provider' under the VPPA when video content delivery is significantly tailored to and essential for its business model, even if the videos are used to facilitate other services like real estate transactions.

A user can be a 'subscriber' under the VPPA without payment if they register for an account and provide personal information, establishing a relationship materially different from casual web browsing.
Allegations that tracking technologies are embedded in websites and intercept communications 'whenever' users interact with content are sufficient to plead the 'in transit' requirement under CIPA.

cases.summary: Second Circuit reversed dismissal of VPPA claim, holding that signing up for NBA's online newsletter by providing personal information made plaintiff a 'subscriber of goods or services' under the Act.

holdings.text: The phrase 'goods or services' in the VPPA's definition of 'consumer' is not limited to audiovisual goods or services, and an online newsletter qualifies as a 'good or service' under the statute.

A person can be a 'subscriber' under the VPPA without paying money by providing valuable personal information in exchange for access to services, establishing a sufficient relationship with the provider.

Alleged unauthorized disclosure of personal viewing information to third parties for targeted advertising purposes constitutes a sufficiently concrete injury closely related to public disclosure of private facts to confer Article III standing.

cases.summary: Class action against GoodRx and tech companies for sharing health data through tracking pixels survived motion to dismiss on most claims.

holdings.text: At the motion to dismiss stage, plaintiffs need not allege specific technical details about tracking technologies' inner workings that only defendants would possess
Health-related communications with medical providers constitute confidential communications under CIPA
Broad privacy policy disclosures about general data collection, when read alongside specific promises not to share health data, do not establish consent to health data sharing as a matter of law
The discovery rule can apply to highly technical privacy violations where plaintiffs allege they could not have discovered the conduct without the benefit of discovery

cases.summary: District court dismissed CIPA claims against Ford for website chat interception, finding insufficient allegations that third-party LivePerson acted beyond Ford's agent and that internet chats don't qualify as telephone communications under section 632.7.

holdings.text: Under CIPA section 631(a), importing a 'use' requirement into the second clause would render the third clause superfluous, and the focus should be on whether the third party has the capability to use intercepted communications for its own purposes rather than the third party's actual use or intentions.

CIPA section 632.7 applies only to communications between various types of telephones and does not encompass web-based messages sent from internet browsers of smartphones, even when one party uses a smartphone to access the internet.

To establish aiding and abetting liability under CIPA section 631(a), a plaintiff must allege that the defendant knew the third party's conduct constituted a breach of duty, not merely that the defendant knew the third party was capturing communications for the defendant's benefit.

cases.summary: Court partially granted motion to dismiss in session replay case, allowing CIPA wiretapping claims to proceed but dismissing other privacy claims for lack of private right of action or protected interest.

holdings.text: Under CIPA § 631(a)(ii), routine website data such as keystrokes, clicks, IP addresses, and browsing information does not constitute 'contents' of communications, which refers only to the intended message conveyed by the communication.
Privacy policies disclosed via hyperlinks without requiring user consent do not establish user consent to third-party monitoring under CIPA's participant exception.
CIPA § 637.2 does not provide a private right of action for violations of CIPA § 635, as the plaintiff must be injured by a violation, not merely by the manufacture or sale of surveillance devices.

Federal Wiretap Act § 2520(a) does not provide a private right of action for violations of § 2512(1), as the civil remedy covers only interception, disclosure, or use, not manufacturing of surveillance devices.
Users do not have a cognizable privacy interest under California's Constitution in browsing data collected only while interacting with a defendant's own website, as opposed to cross-site tracking.

cases.summary: Court dismissed CIPA wiretapping claim against Build.com, finding plaintiff failed to adequately allege third-party eavesdropping by Salesforce in website chat feature.

holdings.text: Under CIPA § 631(a), Clause One does not apply to internet communications, as the statute's plain language limits coverage to 'telegraph or telephone' communications.

To state an aiding and abetting claim under CIPA § 631(a) Clause Four, plaintiff must plausibly allege that a third-party service provider violated one of the first three clauses and that defendant aided that violation.

The party exemption under CIPA does not extend to third-party software providers who have the capability to use recorded communications for their own purposes, following the reasoning in Javier v. Assurance IQ.

cases.summary: Court dismissed CIPA claim against Taro Pharmaceuticals for failure to adequately plead that third-party chat software intercepted communications 'in transit.'
cases.caseName: Heiting v. Taro Pharmaceuticals USA, Inc.

holdings.text: To avoid CIPA's party exception in aiding and abetting cases involving third-party software, a plaintiff must plausibly allege that the third-party software provider has the capability to use communications for a purpose independent of the defendant website owner.

Under CIPA Section 631(a)'s second clause, allegations that communications were intercepted 'as they occur' are insufficient without factual details about the method or nature of interception to satisfy the 'in transit' requirement.
A violation under CIPA's third clause (use of unlawfully obtained information) is contingent upon a finding of a violation of the first or second clause.

cases.summary: Court dismissed CIPA claims against American Eagle for allegedly recording website chat conversations, finding the first clause doesn't apply to internet communications and the company qualified for party exemption under the second clause.
cases.caseName: Licea v. American Eagle Outfitters, Inc.

holdings.text: The first clause of CIPA Section 631(a) applies only to communications over telephones and not through the internet, even when accessed via smart phones, as smart phones are 'in reality, small computers' despite containing telephonic capabilities.

A party to a communication is entitled to the party exemption under CIPA Section 631(a) when third-party software operates more like a tape recorder or tool for the defendant rather than as an independent eavesdropper with separate interests.

To state a claim under the second clause of CIPA Section 631(a), plaintiff must allege specific facts about how and when interception occurs during transmission, not merely conclusory allegations that messages were intercepted 'during transmission and in real time.'

cases.summary: District court partially granted Old Navy's motion to dismiss CIPA claims over website chat recording, allowing eavesdropping claim to proceed but dismissing direct wiretapping liability.

holdings.text: Under CIPA's party exception, a participant in a conversation cannot be held liable for wiretapping another participant, thus Old Navy as a party to the customer chats could not be directly liable under section 631(a).

CIPA Section 632.7 applies to internet-based communications conducted via smartphone when one party uses a cellular device and there is a reasonable expectation of privacy, even if the other party is not using a qualifying telephone device.

To establish derivative liability under CIPA section 631(a), plaintiff must provide facts suggesting that third parties recorded customer information for independent use beyond simply supplying information back to the defendant.

cases.summary: Court dismissed CIPA claims against Sephora for recording website chats, ruling the party exception protects direct communication participants and section 632.7 doesn't cover internet communications.

holdings.text: Under CIPA section 631(a), the 'party exception' protects direct participants to communications from wiretapping liability, as only third parties can 'secretly' listen to conversations between other parties.
CIPA section 632.7 applies only to communications between specific types of telephone devices and does not extend to internet-based chat communications, even when accessed via smartphone.
For CIPA section 631(a) clause 2 violations, communications must be intercepted 'during transmission' and 'in transit,' not after completion or while in storage.

cases.summary: District court dismissed CIPA claims against Ford for website chat interception, finding insufficient allegations that third-party LivePerson acted beyond Ford's agent and that internet chats don't qualify as telephone communications under section 632.7.

holdings.text: Under CIPA section 631(a), importing a 'use' requirement into the second clause would render the third clause superfluous, and the focus should be on whether the third party has the capability to use intercepted communications for its own purposes rather than the third party's actual use or intentions.

CIPA section 632.7 applies only to communications between various types of telephones and does not encompass web-based messages sent from internet browsers of smartphones, even when one party uses a smartphone to access the internet.

To establish aiding and abetting liability under CIPA section 631(a), a plaintiff must allege that the defendant knew the third party's conduct constituted a breach of duty, not merely that the defendant knew the third party was capturing communications for the defendant's benefit.

cases.summary: Court denied motion to dismiss CIPA claims against AI voice assistant company that recorded restaurant orders, finding it was third party with capability to use data for own purposes.

holdings.text: Under the capability approach, a software provider is a third party subject to CIPA section 631 if it has the capability to use intercepted communications for its own purposes beyond merely serving its clients, even if it also functions as a service provider

Allegations of sharing personally identifiable information and personal financial information during a phone call are sufficient to establish a confidential communication under CIPA section 632 at the pleading stage
AI voice assistant software that intercepts phone calls and directly engages with callers in real-time constitutes interception of communications 'in transit' under CIPA section 631

cases.summary: Court dismissed CIPA claim over IP address collection, finding plaintiff lacked standing because IP address disclosure doesn't constitute concrete harm under Article III.

holdings.text: The collection and sharing of an IP address alone, which only reveals general geographic information and cannot personally identify the user, does not constitute a concrete injury sufficient for Article III standing as it does not bear a sufficiently close relationship to traditionally recognized privacy harms such as public disclosure of private facts or intrusion upon seclusion.

California Penal Code § 638.51 does not codify a substantive privacy right but merely limits the manner in which information can be collected, making its violation a 'bare procedural violation' that requires additional concrete harm to establish standing.

A person has no reasonable expectation of privacy in an IP address, consistent with Fourth Amendment jurisprudence, as an IP address is merely a string of numbers associated with a device that accessed a network.
